title: Chapter 17: The Bleed
content: I wake up to the sound of cats.
Not one cat. Cats. Plural. A whole chorus of them, mewling and yowling like a back alley in Marseille at midnight. I sit up in bed, heart slamming, and scan my apartment for the source.
Nothing.

My Murray Hill studio is exactly as I left it — same beige walls the landlord promised were "warm ecru," same IKEA KALLAX shelf with the wobble I keep meaning to fix, same pile of laundry on the chair that I've been not-doing for twenty-one days. The morning light cuts through the blinds in familiar slashes. My coffee mug from last night sits on the nightstand, a ring of residue dried brown at the bottom.
Everything looks right.
But I can *smell* the sea.

Not a hint. Not a suggestion. Full olfactory assault — salt thick enough to coat my tongue, kelp rotting on wet stone, rain hitting slate so hard the minerals rise. I can taste the mineral edge of it at the back of my throat, how a harbor tastes right before a storm rolls in. It's so vivid I almost check my feet for sand.
I don't own a cat. I've never smelled the sea in this apartment. Murray Hill is the opposite of the ocean — it's landlocked mediocrity wrapped in prewar brick.
My hand finds my phone on automatic. I open the Fork app — and the home screen loads like a flatline.
**INTEGRITY: 49%**
**⚠️ CRITICAL WARNING: Timeline boundaries substantially degraded. Spontaneous sensory crossover events increasing. Recommend immediate cessation of all shifting activity.**
*Recommend.*

Not require. Not lock me out. Not shut the whole thing down with a helpful "for your safety" message. Just *recommend*, like a doctor suggesting I cut back on caffeine while I'm already in ventricular fibrillation.
I haven't shifted. I'm in base reality. I went to sleep in my own bed, in my own timeline, with my own mediocre thread count. This isn't a shift. This is something leaking *through*.
A bleed.

I try to close the app. My thumb hits the X and nothing happens. I press harder. The X registers the tap — I can feel the haptic feedback — but the app doesn't close. I swipe up to force-quit. The app minimizes, and then it's back. Front and center. Like it never left.
"Fine," I say out loud, because apparently I'm arguing with my phone now. "I'll delete you."
I press and hold the icon. It wobbles — that familiar iOS jiggle that means you're about to remove something from your life. I hit the minus sign. The icon dims.
Then it snaps back.
Bright. Present. Fixed on my screen like it was pinned there with a nail.

The rules. They come back to me in Julian B's voice, the way he read them from world.yaml in the Clonakilty kitchen, wine in his hand, fire in the grate: *「The app cannot be deleted. It always returns.」*

I knew this. I read it. I nodded along like it was an interesting technical constraint and not a prison sentence. But reading a rule and *experiencing* it are different animals. Reading it, I thought: okay, so I'll always have access. How convenient. Experiencing it — watching my own finger press the button and the universe say *no* — I understand for the first time that the app isn't a tool.
It's a tether.
And I'm on the other end.
I go to work.

This is maybe the stupidest decision I've ever made, and I once ate gas station sushi in a timeline where the health code doesn't exist, so the bar is not low. But I don't know what else to do. I can't call in sick — I've used all my sick days and most of my plausible-deniability days. I can't tell HR I'm experiencing inter-timeline sensory bleed. I can barely admit it to myself.
So I put on a blazer and I go.

The elevator in the lobby is crowded. I press 14 and stand in the back, watching the floor numbers climb. The man next to me sneezes, and I glance over to say bless you — and the words die in my throat.
He has Julian C's scar.

That specific scar. The one that runs from his left eyebrow to his temple, silver-pale and slightly raised, like a river on a map. I know that scar. I've traced it with my fingertip in a timeline where Julian Vance fell down the stairs as a boy and carries the mark like a birthright.

This man is not Julian C. He's fifty-something, receding hairline, khakis. He's never been a version of Julian Vance in his life. But the scar is *there*, identical down to the slight fork at the eyebrow where the stitches weren't quite even.
I look away. My hands are sweating. The elevator dings at 14 and I shove through the doors before they're fully open.

In the bathroom, I splash cold water on my face and drink from the tap. The water hits my tongue and it's — *Sancerre*. Cold, mineral, with that specific chalk-dry finish of a Pouilly-Fumé. I can feel the effervescence on my palate, the way a good Sancerre opens with green apple before it deepens into something almost flinty. I'm drinking New York City tap water from a bathroom sink and my mouth thinks it's a thirty-euro glass of Loire Valley wine.
I grip the porcelain and stare at myself in the mirror. My pupils are blown. There's a vein in my left temple I've never noticed before, pulsing visibly.
"Get it together," I whisper.

At my desk, I open the Meridian file. The numbers swim. Not metaphorically — they literally rearrange themselves on the screen, the neat columns of financial projections breaking into wave patterns. I blink. They settle back into rows. I blink again. The rows become tide charts. Clonakilty Harbor, low tide at 14:32, high tide at 20:17, mean spring range 3.4 meters.

I know these numbers. Not because I'm a sailor — because I've spent the last several shifts in Clonakilty and the harbor schedule is printed on every pub wall. Those numbers don't belong on a Meridian financial spreadsheet. But my brain is *putting* them there, overlaying one reality onto another like tracing paper.
And then I hear Julian B's voice.
*「Reste.」*
Stay.

Clear as a bell. Close enough to feel his breath. I spin in my chair — no one. Raj two desks over is eating a protein bar. Lauren from Compliance is walking past with a coffee. No one reacts. No one heard it but me.

He said it in the kitchen in Paris, that first morning. I was reaching for my coat and he caught my wrist and said *reste* like it was the only word he knew, and I stayed, and that was the beginning of everything that's now unraveling.
I clutch the edge of my desk hard enough to hurt.
My phone buzzes.
**Julian Vance**
I almost don't answer. Almost. But not answering Julian A feels like the kind of thing that makes everything worse, and "worse" is already the baseline.
"Chen." His voice comes through the line clipped and strain-tight. "My office. Now."
Not a question. Not a request. The line goes dead.

I walk to his office on legs that don't feel like mine. The hallway is too long. The carpet pattern is the same it's always been — corporate gray with a thin burgundy line — but it reminds me of the carpet in the Hotel des Arts in Paris and I have to blink the association away.
His door is open. He's standing at the window with his back to me, hands in his pockets, the Manhattan skyline arranged behind him like a backdrop he doesn't see.
I stop in the doorway. "You wanted to see me?"
He doesn't turn around. "Close the door."
I do. The click of the latch sounds like a vault sealing.
"I got a call this morning," he says. Still facing the window. Still not looking at me. "From a woman named Colette Laurent."
The floor tilts. Actually tilts — I feel it under my feet, a fractional list, like the building has shifted on its foundation. I grab the back of the guest chair.
"She says she's your supervisor at the Sorbonne," he continues. His voice is level, almost eerily so, like he's reading a deposition. "She wanted to confirm your thesis schedule."
Colette.

Timeline B. My academic advisor in a life where I went to Paris instead of Wall Street. Sharp-featured woman, sixtyish, who drinks espresso at eleven PM and thinks my thesis on semiotic drift in post-structuralist text is "promising but undisciplined." I never — I *never* gave her Julian's number. I never gave anyone in Timeline B any contact information from this reality. That's the first rule I actually followed. The one rule I didn't break.
How did she get this number?
How did she call across timelines?

The bleed isn't sensory anymore. It's not just me tasting wine in my water and smelling harbors in my bedroom. People from one timeline are calling people in another. The walls aren't just thin. They're *permeable*. And Colette just walked through one.
Julian A turns around.

His face is not angry. That's what throws me. I expected anger — I've seen his anger, the cold clean blade of it. This is worse. He's pale. The kind of pale that comes from real fear, not bad lighting. His jaw is tight but his eyes are searching mine like he's trying to read a language he almost speaks.

"There is no Sorbonne program in your file, Avery." He says my name like it's evidence. "You work here. You've never mentioned studying in Paris. You've never mentioned Paris at all, except that one time you said you'd never been, which is apparently a lie, because someone from the Sorbonne thinks you're their student."
I open my mouth.
Nothing comes out.

He's right. All of it. I work here. There's no Sorbonne in my file. I've never mentioned studying in Paris because in *this* timeline, I didn't. In this timeline, I went to NYU and majored in finance and took this job because the student loans were breathing down my neck and the salary had three more zeros than any PhD program. That's the truth of Timeline A. That's the only truth that fits in this room.

But there's another truth — the one where I did go to Paris, where Colette Laurent is my advisor, where I live in a garret apartment with bad heating and write about Derrida at three in the morning and fell in love with a man who says *reste* like a prayer. That truth exists too. It's just not supposed to exist *here*.
"So explain to me," Julian A says, and his voice drops, and the fear in it is so raw I almost flinch, "why someone from the Sorbonne thinks you're their student."
I can't.

I literally — *literally* — cannot explain this in a way that makes sense inside a single timeline. I'd have to tell him about the app. About the forks. About how I've been living alternate versions of my life for weeks and bringing back pieces that don't fit. I'd have to tell him that the woman who called him is real, and so is my job here, and so is Julian B, and Julian C, and none of them are supposed to be in the same room. Or the same phone call.

I stand there with my mouth open like a broken thing. My brain is scrambling — running through explanations, discarding each one. A misunderstanding? No, Colette is too precise. A prank? Why would a French academic prank a New York hedge fund? A shared name? There's only one Avery Chen at the Sorbonne and she's me, and she isn't, and she is.
Julian watches the scramble.

His expression shifts — half fury, half fear. And I realize, with a clarity that cuts through the static, that the fear isn't for himself. He's not afraid of what I might be hiding. He's afraid of *me*. Not afraid I'll hurt him. Afraid because something is breaking inside me and he can *see* it. He can see the fracture lines spreading and he doesn't know what's on the other side and the not-knowing is killing him.
"Avery." He steps toward me. "Talk to me."

And I almost do. The words are right there — *there's an app, and it lets me live other lives, and I've been doing it for weeks and I can't stop and it's breaking everything*. They're right there, stacked on my tongue like dominoes.
But what comes out instead is: "I can't."
His jaw hardens. The fear doesn't leave his eyes. "Can't or won't?"
"Both."
Silence. The kind that fills a room like smoke.
"Get out," he says. Quiet. Not cruel. Just done.
I run.

Not metaphorically, not emotionally, not in some literary sense where "running" means I buried my face in my hands and processed my feelings. I *run*. I grab my bag and I walk out of his office and then I'm past the trading floor and the numbers and the screens and someone says my name and I don't stop and the stairwell door bangs open under my hands and I'm going down, down, fourteen flights in heels I should've kicked off but I don't stop to kick them off, I just run, my feet slamming against concrete, the sound echoing off walls that smell like industrial cleaner and cold metal.

The stairwell is freezing. The air conditioning vents blast from every half-landing, and the cold hits my bare arms and my neck and it feels like — God, it feels like standing on the harbor wall in Clonakilty in November, the Atlantic wind cutting through my jacket, my ears stinging.
I burst through the ground-floor exit into Manhattan sunshine. Bright, hard, June sunlight that should be warm. That *is* warm — I can see it on the sidewalk, the heat shimmer off the asphalt.
But it feels like Irish rain on my skin.

Cool and wet and mineral-heavy, the kind of rain that isn't really raining so much as existing, hanging in the air so fine you don't notice you're soaked until you try to wipe your face and your hand comes away dripping. That's what Manhattan feels like right now. Sunbeams and taxis and rain that isn't there.

I walk fast. Then faster. Then I'm running again, weaving through Midtown pedestrians who look at me like I'm crazy, and maybe I am, maybe that's what this is now, maybe the bleed is just psychosis and the app is a delusion and everything I've experienced is a break with reality that started when I—
I don't know when it started. I don't know anything anymore.
I run until I'm at Mika's door.

She opens it on the second knock. She's in a paint-stained T-shirt and boxer shorts, her hair twisted up in a claw clip, a brush in one hand. She was painting. Sundays are her painting days. The apartment smells like turpentine and cinnamon — she always burns a candle while she works.
She takes one look at me.
Her smile dies.
"Oh, Aves." Her voice drops to nothing. "What did you do?"

I don't answer because I can't. I walk past her into the apartment and collapse on her couch. The cushions catch me — soft, familiar, the same IKEA EKTORP she's had since college, covered in a mustard-yellow throw that her grandmother crocheted. I sink into it. I sink into the smell of turpentine and the give of the cushions and the sound of Mika closing the door, and I keep sinking.
She sits beside me. Not across from me. *Beside*. Her hand finds my back and stays there, warm and steady.
"Avery." Her voice is gentle but not soft. Mika doesn't do soft. She does *present*. "You have blood on your face."
I touch my nose. My fingers come away red. A nosebleed. I didn't even notice. When did that start?
She hands me a tissue from the box on the coffee table. I press it to my nose. The paper is rough and real and specific in a way that nothing else has been this morning.
"Tell me," she says.
And for the first time — for the first time since I opened that app and saw the fork in the road and chose *both* paths — I tell someone everything.

The app. The shifts. The rules. Timeline B and the Sorbonne and Colette and Julian B with his kitchen in Paris and his *reste*. Timeline C and Clonakilty and Julian C and the harbor and the scar. The integrity counter, now at 49%. The cats that aren't there. The sea I can smell. The Sancerre I can taste. The rain I can feel on skin that's standing in Manhattan sunshine. And Colette — Colette calling Julian's office this morning, crossing from a life that isn't supposed to touch this one, breaching the wall I thought I was keeping between everything.

It all pours out of me like water through a crack. Because that's what I am now. A crack. A fissure with two lives running through me and the pressure building on both sides and the wall between them getting thinner by the hour.

Mika doesn't interrupt. She doesn't gasp. She doesn't tell me I'm crazy or ask if I've seen a doctor or reach for her phone to call someone. She just sits beside me with her hand on my back and listens.
When I'm done, the apartment is quiet. Just the hum of her fridge and the distant sound of traffic on Houston Street.
Mika takes a breath.
"Okay," she says. "Okay."
And the worst part — the part that makes the anxiety bloom in my chest like something with thorns — is that she doesn't sound surprised.
She sounds like she's been waiting for this.
The integrity counter is at 49%. Below halfway. And somewhere in Paris, Colette Laurent is reaching for her phone again.
